content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_1">, opposed to the time of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">actio prima</foreign>, which he has just referred to. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uno_genere_1"> ,<gloss>this one class</gloss> of crimes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tot_dicam_1">: §<ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a) ; B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 533 (467, iii, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tenerem_1">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>,j (287, i); B. 268,7, b; G. 511, R.2; H. 547, I (495, i) ; cf. H.-B. 481. <pb n="324"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_re_1">, etc.: Cicero has now arrived at the climax of his accusation: the case of Gavius is so outrageous that it would require all his powers to characterize it. But, he says, he has already used the strongest language of which he is master in describing other and less heinous crimes, and he has not attempted to keep the attention of the jurors by variety in the charges What then can he do to make this horrible case, the most abominable of the crimes of Verres sufficiently impressive? There is but one thing left to do he will <gloss>tell the bare facts</gloss>, which need no eloquence to emphasize them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_1"> (emphat.), <gloss>the bare facts</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_medio_1"> ,<gloss>before you</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_numero_2"> Cicero has been describing the treatment of a number of fugitives from the insurrectionary army of Sertorius in Spain who had made their way to Sicily after the death of Sertorius, <date when="-0072">B.C. 72</date>, and the overthrow of his faction by Pompey </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lautumiis_2"> ,<gloss>the stone pits</gloss> (ancient quarries) at Syracuse used as a prison. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Messanam_2"> now (<gloss> Messina</gloss>) the point of Sicily nearest Italy Messana founded as a Greek colony in the eighth century B.C., was at this time one of the very few privileged towns (<foreign xml:lang="lat">civitates foederatae</foreign>) of Sicily. It was specially favored by Verres, and, according to Cicero, was an accomplice of his iniquities. Fig. 15 shows a representation of the <gloss> pharos</gloss> (lighthouse) of Messana from a coin of Sex. Pompey ; the reverse has a representation of Scylla. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Reginorum_2">: Rhegium is almost in sight of Messana </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="odore_2"> ,<gloss> breath</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recta_2">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">via</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_praetorio_3"> ,<gloss>the house</gloss> (or <gloss>official residence</gloss>)<gloss>of the</gloss> praetor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adjutricem_3">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 282">282</ref>, c (184, ,b); B. 169,3; G. 321; H.393, I (363, I); H.-B. 319. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magistratum_Mamertinum_3"> ,<gloss>a magistrate of Messana</gloss>: the city of Messana had been treacherously taken possession of by a body of mercenaries, who called themselves <gloss>Mamertini</gloss> (children of Mars), about <date when="-0282">B.C. 282</date>. Though the name of the city was not changed, its citizens were from this time called <gloss> Mamertini</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exspectabant_4"> ,<gloss>were on the watch to see</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_tandem_4"> ,<gloss>how far</gloss>:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tandem_4"> (as also </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nam_4">) gives a sense of <gloss>wonder</gloss> to the question. <pb n="325"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="expediri_4"> ,<gloss>to be got ready</gloss>, i.e. by untying the <foreign xml:lang="lat">fasces</foreign> (rods and axe), which were the badge of the praetor’s <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meruisse_4"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">stipendia</foreign>),<gloss>served</gloss> as a soldier. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="negotiaretur_4">, i.e. as head or agent of some house engaged in speculation (cf. Verr. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.20">20</ref>). This kind of business was generally carried on by Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat"> equites</foreign>, and on a large scale. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fugitivorum_4"> ,<gloss>runaway slaves</gloss>, who had been concerned in the frightful servile war of Spartacus, <date from="-0073" to="-0071">B.C. 73-71</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_4">: subj. of characteristic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="caedebatur_5">: observe the emphatic position. This imperf. and those following make a lively description of the scene instead of a mere statement of the facts. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audiebatur_5"> ,<gloss>could be</gloss> <gloss>heard</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, f (277, g); G. 233. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commemoratione_5"> ,<gloss> claim</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pestem_5"> ,<gloss>accursed thing</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lex_Porcia_6">: this forbade the scourging of citizens. <pb n="326"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="leges_Semproniae_6"> (of Caius Gracchus): these gave Roman citizens the right of appeal to the judgment of the whole people in capital cases, even against the <gloss>military imperium</gloss>. In <gloss>civil</gloss> life this right had existed ever since the foundation of the republic. Cf., in English law, the right of trial <q rend="double">by one’s peers.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribunicia_potestas_6"> see note on Veri 1 sect 44 (p 43,1 3 ) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_inhibebat_6"> Cf note on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audiebatur_6"> (1 1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agere_7">, <gloss> treat</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_7"> (interrog), <gloss> how</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Glabrionem_7"> subject of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facere_7"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_dimitteret_7"> result clause, in appos with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_7"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilium_7"> <gloss> jury</gloss> he feared that the lynch law would get the start of a legal verdict </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="repetisse_7"> ,<gloss> inflicted</gloss> (lit. <gloss>exacted</gloss>, punishment being regarded as <gloss>a forfeit</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veritus_esset_7"> has for its subject </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populus_Romanus_7">. Observe the exactness of tense-relations expressed by the pluperf. and the periphrastic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_persoluturus_7"> ,<gloss>was not likely to pay</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_sit_8"> ,<gloss>what will happen to you</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gavium_istum_8"> ,<gloss>that G. of yours</gloss> (i.e. the G. whom you misrepresent). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="repentinum_8"> ,<gloss>suddenly discovered</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_8">, etc., <gloss>and this I will show</gloss>, etc. Notice that in Latin the connective attracts the negative whenever it can. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquis_8">: Gavius was a very common name in South Italy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_tuum_8"> ,<gloss>at your discretion</gloss> (i.e. as many as you like). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sero_8"> ,<gloss>too late</gloss> (for you, but not too late for the court). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judices_8">, obj. of doceant. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patronis_9">: see note on Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl3.17">p. 3, l. 17</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istuc_ipsum_9"> ,<gloss>that single fact</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nuper_tu_9">, etc.: of course an imaginary incident, since this oration was never delivered. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ideo_9"> ,<gloss>for this reason</gloss>, i.e. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_quaereret_9"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_9">, i.e. after you have said that. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_genere_9">: explained by the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_dicerent_9"> (characteristic). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="induatur_10">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 156">156</ref>, a (111, a) ; B. 175, 2, d; G. 218; H. 407 (377); H.-B. 288, 3; <gloss>tie himself up and strangle himself</gloss> (as in a noose) ; Cf. our <q rend="double">give the man rope enough and hell hang himself.</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_esset_10"> ,<gloss>what he was</gloss> (i.e. whether a citizen or not). <pb n="327"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_ducerere_10"> ,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_clamitares_10">, etc.: in this past condition, cont. to fact, the imperf. is used instead of the pluperf., because the supposition is general rather than particular ; § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, a (308, a) ; G. 597, R.1 ; H. 579, I (510, N.2) ; H.-B. 581; <gloss>if you, caught</gloss>, etc. <gloss>had ever been in the hands of men who were dragging you off to punishment, what other cry would you have raised than, <q rend="double">I am a Roman citizene?</q> </gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profuisset_10"> ,<gloss>would have availed</gloss>, i.e. in the case supposed (as defined in the preceding sentence): thus </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profuisset_10_2"> involves its own protasis; § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref> (311); B. 305, 1; G. 600, 1; H. 575, 9 (507, N.7); H.-B. 582, 4. It is a complete proposition, which is made conditional by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_10">; §<ref n="AG 523">523</ref> (311, d) ; H.-B. 582,4: it is also made the protasis of a new apod., </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_10">, concessive ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_10">, causal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="usurpatione_10"> ,<gloss> claim</gloss> (lit. <gloss>using</gloss> the word). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_11"> =</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_quos_11"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognitoribus_11"> ,<gloss> vouchers</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legum_existimationis_11">, obj. gen. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="periculo_11"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="continentur_11"> ,<gloss>are restrained</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sermonis_societate_11"> ,<gloss>by fellowship in language, rights, and interests</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tolle_12">, a sort of protasis: § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, b (310, b) ; B. 305, 2; G. 598; H. 560, 3 (487, 3); H.-B. 497, 2 ; the apod. is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_12"> . . .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praecluseris_12"> (ll.6-9, below). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_velit_12"> (subj. of integral part), <gloss>any he pleases</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_ignoret_12"> ,<gloss>because one may not know him</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liberas_civitates_12">: the allied states in the provinces, which were not strictly under the jurisdiction of the praetors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praecluseris_12_2">, fut. perf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adservasses_12"> ,<gloss>you might have kept</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="custodiis_12">: abl. of means. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognosceret_12"> ,<gloss>should he know</gloss>: equiv. to a protasis with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_12"> ; §<ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, b (330, b) ; B. 305, 2; G. 598; H. 573, N. (507, iii, i); H.-B. 504, l. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_ignoraret_12">: Cicero here ironically lays down, under the form of a calm and reasonable alternative, the principle that Verres might crucify any Roman citizen whom he did not personally know and who could not furnish a rich man to identify him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_juris_12">: §<ref n="AG 346">346</ref>, a, 3 (216, a, 3); B. 201,2; G. 369; H. 442,1 (397, 3); H.-B. 346. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_tolleretur_12">: clause of purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostis_13">, i.e. by his acts he has virtually declared himself the open enemy of the state as if he were a foreign power <pb n="328"/> making war on the rights of Roman citizens (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">hostis</foreign> rather than <foreign xml:lang="lat">inimicus</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_illi_13">: both words are emphatic, <gloss>it is not to this person</gloss> (in particular) <gloss>but to</gloss>, etc., <gloss>that you were hostile</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_attinuit_13">, etc., for <gloss>what did it have to do with the case that you should order</gloss>, etc.: why should you have ordered, etc., unless by these gratuitous severities you wished to show your hatred of the very name of citizen? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fretum_13">, the <gloss> strait</gloss> of Messina. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="divisa_13"> ,<gloss>thus divided</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alumnum_13"> ,<gloss> foster-child</gloss>, i.e. adopted citizen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facinus_13"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scelus_13"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parricidium_13"> ;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vincire_13"> ,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verberare_13"> ,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necare_13">. For the crucifixion of a citizen Cicero can find no word strong enough ; hence the summit of the climax is reached in quid dicam? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parricidium_13_2">: for the horror with which this crime was regarded by the Romans, see Rosc. Am., sects. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 2.2.28">28</ref>,<ref n="Cic. Ver. 2.2.29">29</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_comitio_13">: i.e. publicly in Rome and in the very centre of Roman freedom and Roman life. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitium</foreign> was an open space north of the Forum, on higher ground (see Plan of Forum, top) ; it was used for the most ancient <foreign xml:lang="lat"> comitia</foreign>, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curiata</foreign> (in which the people were assembled by the thirty hereditary <foreign xml:lang="lat">curiae</foreign>), for hearing lawsuits, and for <foreign xml:lang="lat">contiones</foreign>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat"> curia</foreign>, or Senate-house, fronted toward the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitium</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_13">, i.e. <gloss>that point which</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="celebritate_13">, i.e. as being a crowded thoroughfare. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuit_13">, sc. <gloss> fieri</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetervectione_13">, etc., <gloss>on the track of all who sail to and fro</gloss> (by the Strait of Messina, the necessary route to Greece). </gloss>
